Since this post doesn’t specify an explicit image, the first image in the post will be used in the listing page of posts.

title: Post With Code
text: This is a post with executable code.

title: Ordinary vs Universal Kriging: Why Stationarity Matters
text:
Kriging is a spatial interpolation technique that predicts values at unmeasured locations using nearby known values. It is the same technique oil and gas companies use to predict what lies underground between drill sites. In my project I applied it to NYC traffic sensors to predict traffic volume across the entire city.
But there are two versions of Kriging and picking the right one matters.

title: Ordinary vs Universal Kriging: Why Stationarity Matters
text:
Kriging is a spatial interpolation technique that predicts values at unmeasured locations using nearby known values. It is the same technique oil and gas companies use to predict what lies underground between drill sites. In my project I applied it to NYC traffic sensors to predict traffic volume across the entire city.
But there are two versions of Kriging and picking the right one matters.

title: Ordinary vs Universal Kriging: Why Stationarity Matters
text:
Ordinary vs Universal Kriging
Ordinary Kriging assumes traffic volume has a roughly constant mean across the whole city. It just interpolates locally based on nearby sensors.
Universal Kriging accounts for a city-wide spatial trend in the data before interpolating. It is more complex but more accurate when the mean changes systematically across space.
The choice comes down to one question: is your data stationary?

title: Ordinary vs Universal Kriging: Why Stationarity Matters
text:
Testing for Stationarity
Stationarity means the mean and variance are stable across the study area. I tested this by dividing NYC into four quadrants and running a Kruskal-Wallis test to see if traffic volumes differed significantly between them.
I expected the data to be stationary. I was wrong.

title: Ordinary vs Universal Kriging: Why Stationarity Matters
text:
Results



Time Period
H-Statistic
Result




AM Peak
86.67
Non-stationary


PM Peak
43.77
Non-stationary


Off Peak
85.99
Non-stationary



Traffic volume drops systematically from northwest to southeast NYC. Manhattan and the Bronx have consistently higher volumes than the outer boroughs — denser roads, more commercial activity, more traffic. That is a spatial trend, not random noise, and Ordinary Kriging would have ignored it entirely.

title: Ordinary vs Universal Kriging: Why Stationarity Matters
text:
The Fix
Switching to Universal Kriging with a regional linear drift term told the model to first capture the NW to SE gradient across the city, then interpolate the local variation around that trend. The result is a prediction surface that reflects both the city-wide pattern and the local sensor data.

title: Ordinary vs Universal Kriging: Why Stationarity Matters
text:
Conclusion
Testing for stationarity before fitting a Kriging model is not optional — it determines which version of Kriging is valid for your data. The result surprised me but it made the final prediction surface more honest and more accurate.

title: Moran’s I: Is NYC Traffic Spatially Correlated?
text:
Before you can use Kriging to predict values at unmeasured locations, you need to verify that the data actually has spatial structure which means that nearby locations tend to have similar values. If traffic volume at one sensor had no relationship to the sensors around it, Kriging would be meaningless.
This is where Moran’s I comes in. It is a single number between -1 and 1 that summarizes how spatially autocorrelated your data is:

Close to +1 — nearby locations have similar values (positive autocorrelation)
Close to 0 — no spatial pattern, values are randomly distributed
Close to -1 — nearby locations have opposite values (negative autocorrelation)

For Kriging to be valid you need a positive Moran’s I, confirming that the data has spatial structure worth interpolating.

title: Moran’s I: Is NYC Traffic Spatially Correlated?
text:
Before you can use Kriging to predict values at unmeasured locations, you need to verify that the data actually has spatial structure which means that nearby locations tend to have similar values. If traffic volume at one sensor had no relationship to the sensors around it, Kriging would be meaningless.
This is where Moran’s I comes in. It is a single number between -1 and 1 that summarizes how spatially autocorrelated your data is:

Close to +1 — nearby locations have similar values (positive autocorrelation)
Close to 0 — no spatial pattern, values are randomly distributed
Close to -1 — nearby locations have opposite values (negative autocorrelation)

For Kriging to be valid you need a positive Moran’s I, confirming that the data has spatial structure worth interpolating.

title: Moran’s I: Is NYC Traffic Spatially Correlated?
text:
Computing Moran’s I in SedonaDB
In my project I computed Moran’s I directly from the sensor pair table I had already built in SedonaDB. The key insight is that Moran’s I should use inverse distance weights which means that closer sensor pairs get more influence than distant ones. I also restricted the computation to local neighbours within 2km, since including all pairs across the entire city dilutes the spatial signal.
One thing that tripped me up early on was computing Moran’s I across all sensor pairs regardless of distance. The values came back at around 0.005 — essentially zero — which was alarming. The fix was restricting to local neighbours only, which brought the values up to a much more meaningful range.

title: Moran’s I: Is NYC Traffic Spatially Correlated?
text:
Results



Time Period
Moran’s I
Pairs




PM Peak
0.447
104,370


AM Peak
0.401
145,626


Off Peak
0.366
159,641



All three time periods show moderate positive spatial autocorrelation, confirming that nearby traffic sensors do tend to have similar values. This is the green light Kriging needs to proceed.

title: Moran’s I: Is NYC Traffic Spatially Correlated?
text:
What the Results Tell Us
A few things stand out from these numbers.
First, peak hours have stronger autocorrelation than off-peak. This makes intuitive sense since during rush hour, traffic conditions tend to ripple across the city in a more coherent pattern. An accident or bottleneck on one block affects the blocks around it. Off-peak traffic is more random and localized, so the spatial signal is weaker.
Second, values around 0.4 are considered moderate rather than strong autocorrelation. This is typical for urban traffic data, where road type (highway vs local street) introduces a lot of local variation that has nothing to do with geography. A highway sensor sitting next to a local street sensor will have very different volumes even though they are physically close, which naturally pulls Moran’s I down.
Third, and most importantly, the values are positive and meaningful enough to justify Kriging. The data has spatial structure. Nearby sensors are more similar than distant ones. The prerequisite is satisfied.

title: Moran’s I: Is NYC Traffic Spatially Correlated?
text:
Limitations
Moran’s I gives a single global summary of spatial autocorrelation across the entire study area. In reality, spatial autocorrelation likely varies across different parts of NYC — it might be stronger in Manhattan where the street grid is dense and uniform, and weaker in Staten Island where roads are more dispersed. A local version of Moran’s I called LISA (Local Indicators of Spatial Association) would capture this variation and is something I would explore in future work.

title: Moran’s I: Is NYC Traffic Spatially Correlated?
text:
Conclusion
Moran’s I was an essential first step in validating the Kriging approach for this project. Without it, there would be no guarantee that the spatial interpolation was doing anything meaningful. The values of 0.37 to 0.45 across all three time periods gave me the confidence to move forward with variogram fitting and Universal Kriging, knowing that the data had the spatial structure needed to make the predictions trustworthy.

title: SedonaDB vs GeoPandas: A Spatial Data Showdown
text:
SedonaDB is a new tool specially designed for handling large scale spatial data. This is something that I have been learning about recently and I am very excited since other tools or packages designed for spatial data struggle to handle large scale spatial data. I have been using GeoPandas for a while now and I am pretty familiar with it, but I was excited to hear that there was a new spatial data tool on the rise since I’m not the biggest fan of Pandas or GeoPandas.
In this post, I will be comparing SedonaDB with GeoPandas to see what they have to offer and how they compare to each other. I will be looking at the features, performance, and ease of use of both tools to see which one is better suited for different types of spatial data analysis tasks.

title: SedonaDB vs GeoPandas: A Spatial Data Showdown
text:
SedonaDB is a new tool specially designed for handling large scale spatial data. This is something that I have been learning about recently and I am very excited since other tools or packages designed for spatial data struggle to handle large scale spatial data. I have been using GeoPandas for a while now and I am pretty familiar with it, but I was excited to hear that there was a new spatial data tool on the rise since I’m not the biggest fan of Pandas or GeoPandas.
In this post, I will be comparing SedonaDB with GeoPandas to see what they have to offer and how they compare to each other. I will be looking at the features, performance, and ease of use of both tools to see which one is better suited for different types of spatial data analysis tasks.

title: SedonaDB vs GeoPandas: A Spatial Data Showdown
text:
What are they?
GeoPandas is a Python library that extends the Pandas data management library to support spatial data. It allows you to work with geospatial data in a familiar DataFrame format, and supports reading and writing of common spatial file formats like Shapefiles, GeoJSON, and GeoPackage. GeoPandas runs entirely in memory on a single machine, which makes it simple to use but limits how much data it can handle.
SedonaDB is a spatial computation engine built on top of Apache Sedona. It is worth clarifying that SedonaDB is not just a database it can host and query data locally without needing to set up a server at all. You simply connect to it from Python and start querying straight away:
import sedona.db

sd = sedona.db.connect()

sdf = sd.create_data_frame(df)
sdf.to_view("raw_data")

sd.sql("SELECT * FROM raw_data LIMIT 5").show()
Unlike GeoPandas, SedonaDB is designed from the ground up for large scale spatial data. It uses a SQL interface with built-in spatial functions like ST_Distance, ST_Within, and ST_Transform, making it an easy pickup for anyone already comfortable with SQL. SedonaDB runs as an in-process engine, meaning there is no server to configure or maintain it lives right alongside your Python code.

title: SedonaDB vs GeoPandas: A Spatial Data Showdown
text:
Feature Comparison








Feature
GeoPandas
SedonaDB




Interface
Python API
SQL


Scale
Single machine, in-memory
Large scale, distributed


Spatial joins
Yes
Yes


CRS reprojection
Yes (via pyproj)
Yes (via ST_Transform)


File formats
Shapefile, GeoJSON, GeoPackage
Parquet, GeoArrow, Delta Lake


Visualization
Yes
No (export needed)


Learning curve
Low
Medium


Setup complexity
pip install geopandas
pip install apache-sedona[db]

title: SedonaDB vs GeoPandas: A Spatial Data Showdown
text:
Strengths and Weaknesses

GeoPandas
Strengths:

Very easy to get started with, especially if you are already familiar with Pandas
Excellent visualization support
Large community and extensive documentation
Works well for small to medium datasets that fit in memory
Integrates seamlessly with the rest of the Python data science ecosystem (NumPy, Scikit-learn, etc.)

Weaknesses:

Struggles with large datasets since everything must fit in memory
Have to use Pandas syntax
Not well suited for complex spatial queries that would be natural in SQL



SedonaDB
Strengths:

Designed for large scale spatial data that would crash GeoPandas
SQL interface makes complex spatial queries clean and readable
Built in support for modern columnar formats like Parquet and GeoArrow
Spatial joins and distance computations are highly optimized
Can interoperate with GeoPandas via to_pandas() and create_data_frame()

Weaknesses:

Newer tool with a smaller community and less documentation
No built in visualization — you have to export to GeoPandas to plot
SQL only interface can feel limiting for users who prefer a Python API
Still maturing, so some features and error messages are rough around the edges

title: SedonaDB vs GeoPandas: A Spatial Data Showdown
text:
When to use which?
The choice between GeoPandas and SedonaDB really comes down to the size of your data and the complexity of your spatial queries. For small to medium datasets where you want quick results and easy visualization, GeoPandas is probably the way to go. But when your data grows beyond what fits comfortably in memory, or when you need to run complex spatial joins and aggregations, SedonaDB becomes the better choice.
In my own project analyzing NYC traffic data for spatial interpolation using Kriging, I found that using both tools together worked best. I used SedonaDB to store and query the raw spatial data, compute sensor pair distances, and build empirical variograms tasks that involved millions of point pairs. I then exported the results to pandas for model fitting and visualization. This hybrid approach let me take advantage of the strengths of both tools while avoiding their weaknesses.

title: SedonaDB vs GeoPandas: A Spatial Data Showdown
text:
Conclusion
SedonaDB and GeoPandas are not really competitors they are complementary tools that solve different problems. GeoPandas is the go to for quick exploratory spatial analysis and visualization on datasets that fit in memory. SedonaDB is the better choice when scale, SQL expressiveness, and performance matter. As spatial datasets continue to grow in size and complexity, tools like SedonaDB will become increasingly important, but GeoPandas will remain a staple of the Python geospatial ecosystem for a long time to come.

title: SedonaDB vs GeoPandas: A Spatial Data Showdown
text:
References

SedonaDB Image: https://sedona.apache.org/sedonadb/latest/
